perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement specifies New York law, which is a common choice for business contracts. Changing this to Delaware introduces a potential issue if the parties have no substantial connection to Delaware, and there is no reasonable basis for choosing that state's law. Courts may not enforce a choice-of-law provision if it violates a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest.
perturbation.law_explanation: Choosing Delaware law when neither party is located in Delaware nor has any business in Delaware could be deemed an arbitrary choice. Under conflict of laws principles, courts often look to the state with the most significant relationship to the transaction, and a purely artificial choice of law may not be enforced if it goes against the public policy of the state with a more substantial connection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The change from New York to Delaware law introduces a potential conflict of laws issue if there is no connection to Delaware, which may render the choice-of-law provision unenforceable. As stated in 'Law Snippet 1', conflict of laws refers to 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute.' The 'Perturbation Explanation' states courts may not enforce a choice-of-law provision if it violates a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest. Therefore, changing the governing law to a state with no connection to the agreement could be considered an inconsistency that undermines the enforceability of the contract.

perturbation.original_text: c) Notice. Any notice under this Agreement will be in writing and delivered by personal delivery, express courier, confirmed facsimile, confirmed email or certified or registered mail, return receipt requested, and will be deemed given upon personal delivery, one (1) day after deposit with express courier, upon confirmation of receipt of facsimile or email or five (5) days after deposit in the mail.
perturbation.changed_text: c) Notice. Any notice under this Agreement will be in writing and delivered by personal delivery or standard mail, and will be deemed given upon personal delivery, or ten (20) days after deposit in the mail.
perturbation.explanation: This revision removes express courier, confirmed facsimile, and confirmed email as acceptable methods for delivering notice. It also extends the time for deeming notice given after mailing from five (5) to twenty (20) days. This alteration can create legal inconsistencies because it might not meet the requirements for reasonable notice in certain legal contexts or jurisdictions. Removing confirmed methods like email directly contradicts the UETA.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing 'confirmed facsimile' and 'confirmed email' as acceptable methods for delivering notice and stating that notice is effective 20 days after mailing, the modified text may conflict with the UETA, which provides legal validity to electronic records and signatures. The UETA ensures that electronic communications like email can satisfy legal requirements for notice, which the altered provision undermines.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Archives & Special Collections • Library • Penn Carey Law Skip to main content Home Library » Archives & Special Collections Archives & Special Collections The Archives and Special Collections Department of Biddle Law Library preserves, promotes, and provides access to the papers and records of two major legal organizations: The American Law Institute (ALI), and the National Bankruptcy Archives. The Department also cares for a collection of rare books, manuscripts, and archives from the University of Pennsylvania Carey Law School. Search Center Skip Sidebar Navigation Library Expand mobile section navigation Research Using The Library Faculty Services Archives & Special Collections About the Library News Library Map Library Hours Contact Us Collections The Archives of the American Law Institute (ALI) The ALI was established in 1923 by judges, lawyers, and law professors in an effort to promote the clarification, simplification, and scholarship of the law. The ALI Archives contains over 800 cubic feet of material documenting the administration, organization and work of the Institute, from early projects like the restatement of basic legal subjects to a collaboration with NCCUSL in developing and monitoring the Uniform Commercial Code. View more information about the ALI collections The National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA) In 2000, the Biddle Law Library and the American College of Bankruptcy collaborated to establish the National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA), a national repository of materials relating to the history of debtor-creditor relations, bankruptcy, and the reorganization of debt. The NBA currently contains over 1,000 linear feet of personal papers and institutional records. View more information about the NBA collections Explore the NBA Oral History collections Biddle Collections The Collections consist of manuscript collections, rare books, oral histories, and a select number of Penn Carey Law School’s records. The manuscript collections include the papers of George Wharton Pepper, Bernie Segal, William Draper Lewis, and David L. Bazelon. The Raymond F. Trent Collection consists of books, periodicals, articles, and audio tapes concerning the history of education and practice of Black lawyers in the United States. Some records relating to the operations of Penn Carey Law School are maintained by the University Archives . Please contact us for more information. View more information about the Biddle Law Library Collections Explore the collection of The Report the Law School’s yearbook Explore the Penn Carey Law Oral History Project collection Contact & Hours The Archives and Special Collections office is located on the fourth floor of Tanenbaum Hall. The mailing address is: University of Pennsylvania Carey Law School Biddle Law Library - Archives Department 3501 Sansom Street Philadelphia, PA 19104-3406 For questions about the Archives, the Rare Books Collection, or other special collections, please email the Archives Department at biddlearchives@law.upenn.edu . The Archives is open Tuesday-Friday, 10AM to 4PM. Appointments should be made 2 weeks in advance of the visit. Visitors will be asked to show identification. Policies Registration All researchers are asked to fill out our Researcher Registration Form. This form can be filled out prior to your visit or on the day of your arrival. Archives - Researcher Registration Form On-site Research Items in the Archives do not circulate. If you wish to come to the Archives to conduct research, you may do so free of charge. However, you must contact the department ahead of time to schedule an appointment. Appointments should be mad
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes 'confirmed facsimile' and 'confirmed email' as acceptable methods for delivering notice, and extends the mailing time. The Law Explanation states this conflicts with the UETA, which validates electronic records and signatures. The modified text undermines this validation by removing confirmed electronic methods of communication. Although the scraped snippets are not directly relevant, the explanations provided clearly indicate a contradiction with the UETA by removing electronic communication methods, which the UETA ensures satisfies legal requirements for notice.

perturbation.original_text: Excite will offer Sponsor the right of first refusal to negotiate with Excite for renewal of this sponsorship. 2 3 CONFIDENTIAL i) Excite will not propose, solicit or negotiate offers from entities other than Sponsor for any retail music store sponsorships of the Excite Site, if at all, until [****] prior to the expiration of the term of this Agreement. ii) Commencing not later than [****] prior to the expiration of the term of the Agreement, Excite will negotiate with Sponsor in good faith with respect to the terms and conditions under which this Agreement would be renewed. Excite will negotiate exclusively with Sponsor for the next [****] in good faith effort to negotiate and execute a written sponsorship renewal agreement. If, [****] prior to the expiration of the term of the Agreement, the parties have not entered into a written sponsorship renewal agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Excite will offer Sponsor the right of first refusal to negotiate with Excite for renewal of this sponsorship. 2 3 CONFIDENTIAL i) Excite will not propose, solicit or negotiate offers from entities other than Sponsor for any retail music store sponsorships of the Excite Site, if at all, until one (1) day prior to the expiration of the term of this Agreement. ii) Commencing not later than one (1) day prior to the expiration of the term of the Agreement, Excite will negotiate with Sponsor in good faith with respect to the terms and conditions under which this Agreement would be renewed. Excite will negotiate exclusively with Sponsor for the next zero (0) days in good faith effort to negotiate and execute a written sponsorship renewal agreement. If, one (1) day prior to the expiration of the term of the Agreement, the parties have not entered into a written sponsorship renewal agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By reducing the negotiation period to one (1) day prior to the expiration of the agreement, the modified terms create a situation where the right of first refusal is practically meaningless. This could be deemed unconscionable or a violation of the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in most contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied duty of good faith requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance and enforcement of contracts. Providing only one day to negotiate renewal effectively nullifies the right of first refusal, potentially violating the implied duty of good faith under UCC § 1-304 (even though this is a service agreement, the principle applies).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text significantly undermines the implied duty of good faith by reducing the negotiation period to one day, rendering the right of first refusal practically meaningless. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both highlight this point, arguing that such a short timeframe effectively nullifies the intended benefit of the right of first refusal. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." While this specific snippet refers to the UCC, the Law Explanation correctly notes that the principle applies more broadly. The original text allows for a reasonable negotiation period, whereas the changed text introduces an inconsistency that violates the duty of good faith.
